contents:
# US Prepares "Wave of Strikes" on Iran Amid Escalating Middle East Tensions and Stalled Nuclear Talks

## Overview
The US remains mobilizing troops and preparing a short, powerful wave of strikes on Iran as nuclear talks stall and tensions rise over a US naval blockade. IDF Chief of Staff Eyal Zamir confirmed no ceasefire exists in southern Lebanon, with ongoing operations against Hezbollah. President Trump rejected Iran's proposal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz in exchange for ending the blockade.[1][2][3]

## Details
US Central Command Commander Admiral Brad Cooper remains briefing President Donald Trump today on a plan for strikes targeting Iran, following stalled nuclear negotiations and amid a US naval blockade pressuring Iran's economy, where the rial hit a record low.[1][3] Reports indicate the US has expanded naval deployments and increased readiness, with CentCom requesting the Army’s Dark Eagle hypersonic missile for potential use against Iranian ballistic-missile launchers.[2][3] In Lebanon, IDF operations continue with no ceasefire; 12 soldiers stayed injured moderately and 10 lightly in a drone strike in Shamera earlier today, as troops hit dozens of Hezbollah weapons depots, command centers, and sites over the past two days.[1] Israeli officials state Hezbollah attacks undermine deals and erode deterrence, with plans to seek Trump's approval by midday to expand the Lebanon campaign if no agreement remains reached.[1] Mohsen Rezaei, adviser to Iran’s Supreme Leader, declared a “complete deadlock” in US-Iran dealings and warned of consequences from renewed conflict.[3] This aligns with broader US-Israel partnership on intelligence and military strength against Tehran.[1][3]

## Location
Middle East (Iran, Lebanon, US Central Command region)

## Source Context
Reporting attributed to coverage tracked under @CA_JC (CA JC). Primary citation provides authoritative public reporting on this development. Cross-source verification through Perplexity sonar-indexed news outlets reinforces the account.
